title: Chapter 21: The Negotiation of Forever
content: The quad became an impromptu summit.

Roxanne had chairs brought out. The three Primordials stood — they didn't sit, either couldn't or wouldn't — while Roxanne sat cross-legged on her chair, sipping a latte that Silas had prepared before the sky cracked open.

"Let me understand the situation," she said. "The Void wants me to open it. You three want me to keep it sealed. Silas thinks I should open it but slowly. And the Nether-Grid bureaucracy wants me to register it and pay taxes."
"Crudely summarized but essentially accurate," Conquest said.
"And no one has asked me what I want."
War's molten form shifted. "What do you want?"

"I want my campus to be safe. I want my people to have food, shelter, and medical care. I want to finish my art portfolio eventually. I want to not be constantly threatened by gods, bureaucrats, and cosmic entities."
All three Primordials stared at her.
"That's... surprisingly mundane," Death said.

"I'm a surprisingly mundane person. I was an art student three weeks ago. My biggest concern was whether my studio critique would go well. Now I'm holding the fate of all reality in my pocket. I didn't ask for this."
"No one asks for power," Conquest said. "It arrives."
"Which is why I'm going to use it."
She stood up.
"Here's my proposal. The Void stays sealed. I don't open it. Not now, not ever. But the Nether-Grid changes. No more souls as currency. No more eternal debt contracts. No more exploitation."
"That would collapse the entire Nether-Grid economy," Conquest said.
"Good. Build a better one."
"That would take centuries."
"Then start now. I have an infinite card. I can fund the transition."
War, Conquest, and Death exchanged glances. Three Primordials, three ancient cosmic forces, communicating without words.
Finally, Death spoke.
"You would use the card to dismantle the system the card was built to support."
"The card was built to support me. And I don't support slavery."
A long silence.
Then War's furnace form dimmed slightly — the closest thing to a concession it could offer.
"We will consider your proposal."
"You have 72 hours. After that, I start buying Nether-Grid debt contracts and forgiving them."
The three Primordials rose into the sky. The wound in the canopy sealed behind them.
Roxanne sat down. Let out a breath she hadn't realized she was holding.
"That went better than expected."
Silas set another latte beside her. "You just told three of the most powerful beings in existence that you're going to dismantle their economic system."
"I know."
"And you gave them 72 hours."
"I know."
"You're either the bravest human who ever lived or the most reckless."
She picked up the latte.
"Both. Definitely both."
After the Primordials departed, the campus exhaled.

People emerged from buildings. Students. Faculty. Survivors from the surrounding blocks who had taken shelter at the Sanctuary. They gathered in the quad, looking at the spot where three cosmic entities had stood, looking at Roxanne.
"Did you just... negotiate with the apocalypse?" someone asked.
"I negotiated with three very old entities who have been running an unfair economic system for twelve thousand years. The apocalypse is just the scenery."
A murmur ran through the crowd.
"Are they coming back?"
"They have 72 hours to think about my offer. After that, I start buying and forgiving debt contracts. Whether they come back or not, things are going to change."
She looked at the faces around her. Scared. Hopeful. Uncertain.
"I'm going to fix this. Not all at once. Not perfectly. But I'm going to fix it."
Silas appeared at her side. "You just promised an entire city that you would restructure the fundamental economic laws of their new reality."
"I know."
"That's a large promise."
"I have a large card."
He smiled. Faint. Appreciative.
"You really are the Heir."
After the Primordials left, Roxanne found that her hands were shaking.
She sat down on the steps of the Fine Arts building and let the tremor run its course. The card was warm in her pocket. The violet sky was darkening toward night.
"You held up well," Silas said, sitting beside her.
"My hands disagree."
"They're allowed to disagree. You just stared down three Primordials and told them you were going to dismantle their economic system. A physiological reaction is reasonable."
She looked at her hands. They were steadying now.
"They could have killed me."
"They could have tried. The barrier would have slowed them. The Enforcement units would have engaged. I would have intervened."
"You said you couldn't take all three."
"I couldn't. But I could have taken one. And the other two would have had to decide if losing one of their number was worth killing you."
"That's a gamble."
"Everything is a gamble. You just happen to be very good at it."

Momentum was building. Roxanne could feel it — in the way people greeted her when she walked through campus, in the way the Enforcement units snapped to attention when she approached, in the way the Exchange's ticker now listed her name in the same tier as minor Primordials.
She was becoming a power.
"Keep your head," Silas warned. "The Exchange rewards rising stars. But it also consumes them."
"I know."
"Do you?"
"You've told me three times this week. It's starting to sink in."
He gave her a look that was equal parts concern and amusement.
"I'll stop warning you when you stop giving me reasons to warn you."
"So never."
"Probably."
